Welcome to Magnetic Marketing Session number three, how to create a compelling message your ideal prospects are powerless to resist. Now when creating a compelling message your goal is to attract just the right person to make them stop and say hey, that product or service is right for me. So Dan, let's discuss exactly how you go about doing that and the perfect place to start with this would be with what is called the USP. What is a USP? Unique selling proposition or unique selling positioning position and I have a copyrighted question for it which is why should I do business with you versus any and every other option available to me in your category including doing nothing. Can you repeat it again a little slower because that was... Why? Session number three. Why should I do business with you versus any and every other option available to me in your category including doing nothing. And so sort of now that you have it then people go well great how do I get one right and so you know there's pathways to a USP but linking to session two backwards the best USPs are always at least in part driven by the target market that has been selected to receive them. Right. And what they want. Exactly. So you know the old example that we've used for years and then I pulled together four or five pathways but the example that I've used for years was the original Domino's USP which built Domino's from you know one failing pizza joint kid going to college to pretty quick to a major player in that industry and it was fresh hot pizza delivered in 30 minutes or less guaranteed. Now I joke about it but it is significant that all of the early Domino's locations including the first one were adjacent to they were in college towns and they were basically adjacent to college campuses. Hence you had now you have everybody doing it you had is interesting how laws change you had buildings full of people all smoking the same illegal substance. One of the side effects was a need for large quantities of carbohydrates in a hurry that did not necessarily have to taste good or taste better than anybody else's carbohydrates. The issue was speed and most of the people who needed them were not in a position to go get them. So 30 minutes or less talking about your 30 minutes or less 30 minutes or less. Guaranteed was a was built for the target market that was being served at the time and you don't have anything in the USP about good great better mama's recipe from the old country only use organic ingredients only use milk from cows who only eat natural stuff. None of that's there because that target market did not doesn't care right. And so the best USPs are at least in part considerate of a target market. Okay. And so I mean pathways if you want to talk about that let's talk about how we get so first of all you think about positioning and differentiation in a market. Can I ask you a question for a while. A lot of people get this mixed up. What's the difference between a slogan versus a USP. A lot of people think they've got a USP but it's really a slogan. A slogan is stupid and a USP is smart. Okay. Fantastic. That's clear. Well so first of all a lot of slogan ism. You see it in big corporations again about which we must be very careful of emulating. In many cases you see it in businesses that are unwilling to actually have a point of differentiation. So you see big financial services companies who who for a variety of reasons are unwilling to actually try and differentiate themselves. So their slogan ism is big broad fuzzy warm feeling and therefore sort of meaningless. But I call vague generalities rather than meaningful specifics. Right. So they are unwilling to say for example we will guarantee you won't run out of money before you run out of life. So they say vaguer broader sloppy your things. Sloganism is almost always product driven not market driven. So it is all about who they are and what they got. We're the oldest we're the best we're the smartest we're the biggest we're the you know. So if you think about Hertz's slogan for years and years and years and years right was we're number one. Right. There's really nothing there that is particularly meaningful to a marketplace. Now to a certain segment of the marketplace there is a customer who always prefers to deal with whoever is number one. Right. But the vast majority of the marketplace doesn't care and there's no benefit in that. There's no consumer promise in that. There's really nothing interesting in that. And to a certain degree it'll also turn off a certain number of people because I don't know that I really like you telling me that you're number one. Right. You know. So that's a slogan. So unique selling proposition is really nationals right now which is beautiful which is pick any car right get in and go and they're targeting the business. Yeah. The business the business. So they have a unique selling proposition. Right. Enterprises is a unique selling proposition will pick you up. Right. So that's the difference between a USP and a slogan. Now I could make any of those better by the way but that's really not the case. So if you look at pathways one is positioning position what it is you do or sell different than everybody else in that category positions it one of the great and ironic and funny examples of our time is what Subway did now 11 years ago or so when Jared wandered in their door and somebody was not so corporate stupid has to pat him on the head and send him on his way. And so Subway morphed changed their positioning from another fast food alternative to a weight loss program. So they have managed to convince people that a foot long loaf of bread cut in half with processed lunch meat and a few vegetables is weight loss food. Right. Right. Which is remarkable that they have pulled it off very recently Taco Bell tried it with far less success. But they've done it with Subway and it's had legs it's lasted for a decade. Right. And it's kind of has a completely evaporated now. No. And it's kind of neat if you want to talk about positioning at the time with that we're creating this they've positioned even a little bit more now they have athletes in it. So they've got celebrity spokespeople. So this is what not only what to lose with this is what healthy people right athletes eat. Well this is the official training diet so and so right. Right. You will notice a goodly number of those athletes are no longer competing. They're ex athletes but nonetheless. So another not because they were eating Subway by the way for the maybe eat Subway because they no longer compete but we don't know that. So you're sticking in disclaimers now. I'll put it in this you're actually putting in disclaimers now the corporate influx. So another pathway is speed. Okay. So if you can do it fast all the way back to 30 minutes or less guaranteed. So you see companies like three day blinds. Same day dentures or same day implants in dentistry. Very big thing. The dermatologist I was with the other day their practice the acne scar curative process is proprietary and patented and it requires only two visits and it's named fast scar cure. So that's a speed proposition. Right. A guarantee proposition. Again Domino's had that too 30 minutes or less guaranteed. We have a member who's a very smart marketer and he does all the marketing for men's health clinics. You will see their ads increasingly and more and more marketplaces across the country and men's health clinics primary product service has to do with fixing erectile dysfunction. And their guarantee promises you will have a test experience in the office with the chemical formula we mix up for you and you will know that it works. Right. And no other medical nobody you know it's a it's a unique guarantee position try before you buy. They saw our mattress friends here from Gardner's mattress who are in the front row created a somewhat influenced by another member of ours a co author of mine Sidney Barrows created what they call their dream room. So the normal method of shopping a mattress everybody owns a mattress so you've all done it right you go into a store there's six bazillion mattresses you lay on this one for three minutes you lay on this one for three minutes nine out of 10 times by the third night you're sleeping on it. It didn't feel exactly like it did in three minute trial in the store people are really sort of reluctant to lay on it like they would really lay on it right full view of everybody. So they've created a dream room that for an hour three hours kind of whatever you want. And it looks like it's kind of like a upscale hotel room and you pick the bed they move the bed in there and there's a TV and you go in there and you. Wow. You know you experience a mattress in a real environment for a long enough period of time that it is a legitimate so it's tried before you buy right. There's personal relevance if it is another pathway which gets to message to market match all the way back to the product. So a generic product is a nutritional formula to help your eyesight. If you go into a GNC store there's probably 20 or 30 varieties of vitamin formulations to help protect or improve your vision. There is one company that has a product positioned to protect and improve the vision of airline pilots. Wow. And they market it only to target market to airline pilots via media that are read by airline pilots both commercial and recreational subculture private. So you fly your own plane just because you want to. I fly my plane because I work for American Airlines and it's my career. What we have in common is we lose our license if our eyesight deteriorates. Therefore the criticality is more important to us than it is to somebody that just wants to see television right and be able to find their dog. Right. Therefore they'll pay more for it. Right. So it has price elasticity. Right. Well and also it's for me. Yeah. Exactly. Specifically for me. Now truth be told. Right. It's the same nutrients. Right. Right. And it's the same ratio of right. Right. But because of personal relevance we've taken a generic product and we've given it a unique selling position. Those are some of the pathways. One of the things I want to point out here and I think a lot of people listen to this and their immediate reaction is and this is a lot of people's immediate reaction is well my business is different. This is not going to work for me. How do I apply it if I'm a butcher baker? Well there's actually two really crappy reactions that are possible not just the one you named. So one horrible reaction is well my business is different. So I haven't heard veterinary medicine only for small animals in a small town in the Northwest mentioned. Therefore this doesn't apply to me and unless you tell me there's a template in here for a cat veterinarian in a small town. Right. This isn't for me. So there's that incredibly blind deaf and dumb reaction. The other one is my business is ordinary and mundane and there's no way for me to use unique sales proposition. Which is the reason why you need to use it. Well so there's a cure for that. It's called a noose or a bullet or you know leaping off of a high building you know and pretty much other than the bullet it's free. I mean so you don't have to buy anything in order to get the cure so and pick your poison so to speak. So here so far is what we've named. We've named mattresses, vitamins, sub sandwiches, dentistry, Venetian blinds and pizza. Right. How much more mundane and ordinary would you like to be? Right. The point of getting to a compelling message, a message that cannot be ignored and then to get to an offer that cannot be resisted. But to get to a message that cannot be ignored is taking an ordinary business and finding a way to position it so it is extraordinary and of exceptional interest to somebody. Right. Right. So to sit there and say that you can't do that. Right. Because you sell something ordinary it's exactly the point. And to say that you can't do it because your business is different. Well find one that this hasn't touched. Right. You name me one. Right. So I find you someone who has found a way to make it extraordinary and to target point it. So what's more mundane than coffee? Right. There's some overlap of customer. But by and large the Starbucks customers a different customer than the Dunkin Donuts customers who's a different customer than the Denny's customer. There are different customers than the person who stops every day to get their coffee at the convenience store. Right. It's coffee. Right. It's brown water. Right. What is it called? Sivit. So Sivit coffee is like 60 to 80 dollars a pound. Right. And it is coffee grounds that are made from cat poop. Coffee beans are eaten by a cat and pooped out the other end by a cat. And that's turned into coffee that people want to buy for 60 to 80 dollars a pound and brew. And who wouldn't. There's well. You probably wouldn't. I probably wouldn't. But there are people who. But there are enough people to do it that there's at least six companies making it. Right. So let's get somebody past that. Why is this important? Well how does this change the business if I get this right? Well it can allow you to sell coffee for six dollars a cup as in Starbucks's case. Right. So it can give you price and profitability advantage. In a world that is all about price suppression to the point of bankruptcy. Right. And so we see in category after category after category we see price suppression these days. Right. If you look at what I paid for my first car and you look at what you can go buy a comparable car for today and you calc it against the inflation that has occurred during that period of time the price has gone down not up. Right. And they're going down. If you look in our world even in the seminar world. Right. And you look at so I just looked at it the other yesterday for a particular reason. So in 2002 my seminar for chiropractors was $2,495 a person to attend 2002. Most marketers of seminars to chiropractors today are priced under $1,000. Right. So it is the rate of inflation they've actually gone backwards. Right. So it can help you with price. It can help you with customer attraction by being profoundly for certain people rather than being sort of for everybody which means you're sort of for nobody. Sure. Right. This is not for me. Right. Right. But if you got five kids. Right. And they're all growing and eating. Right. Right. So like my daughter has three and they're all growing and eating. Right. Costco is perfect for her. Right. And so it allows you to be for somebody rather than to be for everybody which largely places you in the world of sort of being for nobody. Absolutely. Then when people are developing so that's just one part of the compelling message which is USP when people are developing their marketing messages. One of the mistakes that they make on if you could just comment on a little bit is they're focused so much on their product on the product as opposed to the result or the outcome that their buyer is going to get. So if you look at almost everybody's advertising in general and in whatever category you're in you're going to find everybody talking about a relatively small range of things. Product or service. What we got what we sell. Features and benefits. It's made out of plastic and that's why it's good. Right. It's stitched instead of glue bound and that's why it's good. Okay. Features and benefits. Right. By the way if you see buy one suit and get forty seven free they're not stitched. They're put together with a glue gun. So product service features benefits price discounts. And eighty ninety percent of the conversation is that. So if we didn't know anything we would know not to do that because eighty to ninety percent of everybody is doing that which a means it's very hard for us to stand out stand apart be noticed and see the majority is always wrong about money. They're poor so we don't want to copy them. Right. So we want to have a different conversation. We either want to start with which I know you're going to get to in segment four the lead generation conversation where we aren't talking really about us at all. Right. Right. Or as you just said we want to get you have an outcome conversation and work backwards. Right. There was laughter in the hinterlings. So they both do the same thing. Right. They're literally all their stuff is all about the things I just named. Right. These guys are talking about they got laser and they got red laser and blue laser and they got deaths and they got that. And worse and here's how much we charge for this and here's how much we charge for that. And it's also horribly premature. Right. And so horribly common because really what I want is the outcome. Right. I want to look 10 years younger than I look now and I want all my scars to be gone and candidly I don't really care how you do it. Right. At least until I know you can do it. Right. So and really I want to bad enough if what you're going to do is hang me upside down by my ankles and a vat of blue gunk and four people are going to stand around and home and that's actually going to do it. It's OK by me. Right. Right. And people don't get that. Right. Business people don't get that because they're so focused on their thing. Right. Right. And not focused on their customer and what that customer wants. Right. Right. Exactly. And as long as we are going to do one let's build an irresistible offer. Right. Which an irresistible offer is exactly what it says. It's got so much cool stuff in it. It's got so much relevant information to the audience. So it's got information value or it's got entertainment value or it's got deliverable value that does not require me to buy your product or service necessarily to get value. It's got all those things added together. If you are selling rather than generating leads then the offer itself so you know one of the greatest old examples is what built Vegas world from the ground up in Las Vegas. So three days two nights at a hotel. ebsite website well so if it's the first answer those are real handcuffs if it's the second answer that's you now being cheap and slothful and stupid so you got to decide where you really are right so like almost everybody tells me about the legal stuff and the compliance stuff they draw a line that is far short of where the actual letter of the law is and when pressed and we discover and we go to compliance departments we find that they've put the line way too short right then if you are totally hamstrung and restricted you just have to decide if the compensating benefits justify living with that and living with essentially bad marketing right so if I own 16 McDonald's franchises and they are perpetual motion money machines so I have a friend who owns a bunch of Wendy's all the franchise owners hate him the corporate advertising is bad they all know it but they're basically lazy they certainly are not going to chuck the thing and start their own and there's a lot of benefits to the corporate parent right fine if there's not enough compensating benefit then you got to think about do I leave the franchise network and become independent do I leave this financial services company and go to a different financial services company that has a different compliance department so you know these are these are legitimate concerns that are often given much more power than they actually have and one of the things that you casually mentioned was use it on the back end so I don't want to give people the impression that magnetic marketing is just for the front end at all because it's
